attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman is brought to the Emergency Department by her concerned partner due to worsening confusion over the past week. She has a medical history of chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications include candesartan, linagliptin, dapagliflozin, insulin, and sevelamer. On examination, she appears drowsy with a pulse rate of 96/min, blood pressure 158/94 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She is disorientated to time and place. There are bibasal crackles on chest auscultation and pitting oedema to the mid-shins. Investigations reveal:

Serum creatinine: 780 μmol/L (45-90)
Serum urea: 44 mmol/L (2.5-6.5)
Serum potassium: 5.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Serum bicarbonate: 16 mmol/L (22-28)
Haemoglobin: 79 g/L (115-160)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Administer one unit of packed red blood cells
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with confusion, disorientation and drowsiness, likely in the context of symptomatic uraemia, due to the progression of chronic kidney disease. The most appropriate next step in management is to perform urgent haemodialysis, to remove the excess wastes causing the uraemic encephalopathy.

Whilst the patient is anaemic, likely at least in part due to end stage kidney disease, performing urgent haemodialysis is the management priority due to the dangerous nature of uraemic encephalopathy. Once stabilised, interventions to raise the patients haemoglobin level can be employed including iron infusions, erythropoietin and packed red blood cells if required.

Haemodialysis will improve the metabolic acidosis (hence administering intravenous sodium bicarbonate is a lesser priority), offload fluid (hence administering intravenous furosemide is a lesser priority and diuretics are often ineffective in some patients with end stage kidney disease), and lower the serum potassium level (hence administering insulin and dextrose is a lesser priority).
learningPoints: Urgent dialysis is indicated in patients with chronic kidney disease presenting with symptomatic uraemia i.e. confusion.

The other acute indications for emergency dialysis are refractory metabolic acidosis, refractory electrolyte disturbances (i.e. hyperkalaemia), intoxicants/toxins, and refractory fluid overload. These can be remembered with the mnemonic: AEIOU.
references: Murdeshwar HN, Anjum F. Hemodialysis [Internet]. PubMed. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563296/
